frameworks.name: Depth-Plus-Breadth Skill Architecture
frameworks.definition: A career model requiring deep mastery in one discipline combined with breadth across related fields; depth alone is insufficient for high-value roles in fast-changing industries.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that career planning is largely a pretense in fast-changing industries: a significant proportion of job titles in London advertising in the 2010s did not exist in 1995, making forward career planning structurally unreliable rather than merely difficult.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
bounded_by: May not apply to highly stable professions (law, medicine) where role definitions are regulated and change slowly

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that the modern high-value employee must be good at more than one thing: either very deep in one discipline combined with breadth across related fields, because depth alone no longer differentiates sufficiently in complex organizational roles.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Single-skill depth is increasingly replicable; breadth enables novel combinations that specialists cannot produce, making the combination rarer and more valuable than either skill alone.
bounded_by: Does not necessarily apply to highly commoditized technical execution roles where narrow depth is the sole requirement

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that early-career randomness and non-linear exploration pay off in the long run by building versatility and breadth, even though the time spent may feel wasted at the time.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Exposure to varied roles accumulates tacit knowledge from each domain; that tacit knowledge becomes applicable in unpredictable future situations, creating compounding value unavailable to narrow early specialists.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that the more recruitment processes attempt to appear fair, methodical, and rigorous by applying the same criteria to everyone, the more homogeneous the resulting hires become, which undermines the agency's actual functioning.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: creative agencies and similar organizations where output quality depends on diverse thinking
claims.mechanism: Uniform selection criteria optimize for a single profile; organizations that require diverse cognitive styles for effective output cannot achieve that diversity if their screening filters out deviation from a standard template.
bounded_by: Does not apply to roles requiring strict regulatory compliance or safety certification where standardization is required

claims.text: Rory Sutherland identifies curiosity as the single most important quality he looks for when hiring for creative roles at Ogilvy, and states that fundamental incuriosity is an absolute deal-breaker for anyone entering an advertising department's creative function.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
bounded_by: Criterion stated specifically for creative advertising roles; Sutherland does not claim it applies to all organizational roles universally

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that human beings largely act first and then construct post-hoc reasons for their actions, meaning the conscious narrative of thinking-then-deciding does not accurately describe the underlying cognitive process.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: A large portion of conscious experience is a confabulation that retrospectively justifies behavior already initiated; the actual causal sequence is action-before-reason rather than reason-before-action.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that while you cannot directly control what you feel is important, you can control where you direct your attention, and this indirectly changes your priorities and what matters to you.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Attention allocation shapes salience; repeatedly directing attention to a domain raises its perceived importance even when no direct volitional change to values or priorities is made.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that mainstream media bears more responsibility than social media for amplifying fringe opinions into apparent mainstream scandals, because journalists harvest extreme minority views from platforms like Twitter and republish them as representative public opinion.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: advertising or brand campaigns visible on social media
claims.mechanism: Prior to social media, journalists had to physically canvass opinions, making extreme outliers hard to locate. Social platforms concentrate extreme commenters in discoverable clusters, enabling journalists to find and amplify rare outrage at negligible cost, creating the false appearance of broad public disapproval.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that mission-based or issue-based advertising walks a fine line: if a significant number of people misunderstand the advertisement, willfully or not, the campaign has a structural problem regardless of intent.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: advertising that references social or political issues
brand campaigns with high public visibility

claims.text: Rory Sutherland proposes that there is a meaningful correlation between a disposition toward idleness and ingenuity: lazy people tend to prefer thinking through simpler solutions rather than solving problems by brute force, which maps onto a form of creative problem-solving.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: A preference for avoiding unnecessary effort creates pressure to find more efficient paths; this is functionally identical to creative problem-solving, which seeks lower-cost routes to the same outcome.
bounded_by: Rory Sutherland presents this as a partial correlation ('some sort of correlation'), not a universal rule

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that the most vital organizational characteristic of an ad agency is a culture where it is safe to fail, propose silly ideas, and still advance in the organization, because innovation requires psychologically safe failure.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: creative agencies and similar innovation-dependent organizations
claims.mechanism: If career advancement requires consistent public success, employees suppress speculative ideas before articulating them; this self-censorship removes the raw material from which breakthroughs emerge.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that advertising and marketing practitioners err when they begin with media choices rather than starting from a deep understanding of human psychology, motivation, and decision-making; the correct sequence is psychology first, then media selection.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland

examples.text: Rory Sutherland described his own career as an illustration of the value of non-linear early-stage paths: he read Classics at Christ College, joined Ogilvy in 1988 as a graduate trainee and was named the worst graduate trainee the agency had ever hired, was fired from the planning department, reapplied, was sent to creative, became a copywriter, and five years later was executive creative director of Ogilvy One, eventually becoming vice chairman of Ogilvy UK. He notes that even time spent as a poor account person remained of value to him decades later, and that a messy early career should carry no shame.

Rory Sutherland used the Pepsi-Kylie Jenner advertisement (circa 2017) to illustrate the dynamics of issue-based advertising and social media outrage. The advertising industry largely identified the ad's problems immediately; outside the industry, most people saw nothing wrong with it. He argued the ad's failure was that strategic intent was visible ('strategic bra straps showing') without earning the credibility to carry that intent, and that the mainstream media then amplified the minority outrage from Twitter into a nationwide scandal.

Rory Sutherland noted that pre-social-media investigative journalism required journalists to physically locate and interview members of the public, which made extreme outlier opinions difficult to source because such people were genuinely rare and geographically dispersed. On Twitter, a journalist seeking outrage about an advertisement can simply search for aggrieved users within minutes and cut-paste their comments into an article, transforming a fringe position into apparent mainstream sentiment.

practices.text: Rory Sutherland recommends that whatever you do in life, aim to be good at at least two interconnected things rather than a single narrow specialty.

Rory Sutherland recommends that organizations hire people first and then work out roles around them, rather than hiring to fill pre-defined positions, in order to attract unusual talent that rigid role specifications would exclude.

Rory Sutherland recommends that marketing and advertising professionals return to the basics of human psychology and understand motivation and decision-making before selecting which media or platforms to use.
